coredata.dc:title: Triple heterojunction photocatalytic composites based on CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S for visible-light-degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Abbassian, Khalil
Rahmani, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Overuse of antibiotics endangers human health by promoting drug-resistant diseases. Using innovative MOF-based composites for gentamicin photo-mineralization is an eco-friendly approach to combat water pollution. A triple heterojunction photocatalytic composite with excellent visible-light harvesting was fabricated via a solvothermal approach based on TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF core-shell structure decorated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods. In addition to narrowing the energy gap and restraining electron-hole recombination, TiO2 (20 mg) embedding in Ti-TPA-MOF and decorating with CQDs (10 %) and Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods (50 ppm of Cu2+ and 48 ppm of Zn2+), due to created high electron conductivity, increases the current density and photodegradation activity of gentamicin compared to pure Ti-TPA-MOF. 150 mg/L of triple heterojunction composites exhibited an efficiency of 6 times compared to Ti-TPA-MOF after 80 min of visible-light irradiation. The findings supported photodegradation reaction pseudo-first-order kinetics. The lack of significant change in efficiency after five photoreaction cycles indicates photocatalytic stability. The use of visible-light-active triple composites, which have great potential for new composite development, is suggested as a promising treatment method.

head.ce:title: Triple heterojunction photocatalytic composites based on CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S for visible-light-degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Overuse of antibiotics endangers human health by promoting drug-resistant diseases. Using innovative MOF-based composites for gentamicin photo-mineralization is an eco-friendly approach to combat water pollution. A triple heterojunction photocatalytic composite with excellent visible-light harvesting was fabricated via a solvothermal approach based on TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF core-shell structure decorated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods. In addition to narrowing the energy gap and restraining electron-hole recombination, TiO2 (20 mg) embedding in Ti-TPA-MOF and decorating with CQDs (10 %) and Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods (50 ppm of Cu2+ and 48 ppm of Zn2+), due to created high electron conductivity, increases the current density and photodegradation activity of gentamicin compared to pure Ti-TPA-MOF. 150 mg/L of triple heterojunction composites exhibited an efficiency of 6 times compared to Ti-TPA-MOF after 80 min of visible-light irradiation. The findings supported photodegradation reaction pseudo-first-order kinetics. The lack of significant change in efficiency after five photoreaction cycles indicates photocatalytic stability. The use of visible-light-active triple composites, which have great potential for new composite development, is suggested as a promising treatment method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics in healthcare can create antibiotic-resistant pathogens and harm aquatic ecosystems [1]. Gentamicin, used for severe infections, can cause renal damage and auditory impairment [2]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a cost-effective strategy to protect aquatic ecosystems. Semiconductor-based photocatalysis has the potential to convert organic pollutants into harmless substances such as CO2 and H2O by utilizing visible light [3,4]. Researchers are interested in exploring composite semiconductors for their effectiveness in degrading pollutants [5–7]. Photocatalytic processes are economically viable and effectively remove contaminants [8–11]. However, conventional semiconductor photocatalysts like TiO2, WO3, and BiVO4 have limitations such as small surface area, limited responsiveness to visible light, and electron-hole pair recombination [12–15].

Photocatalytic MOFs have gained popularity for their semiconducting properties, large surface area, and customizable porosity. Their ease of functionalization sets them apart from metal oxide photocatalysts [16,17]. The porous structure of MOFs allows for effective pollutant adsorption, while open metal sites promote the creation of reactive species [18–20]. Ti-TPA-MOF is a Ti-based MOFs material consisting of terephthalic acid (TPA) ligands and TiO4 clusters. Ti-TPA-MOF is widely used for removing antibiotics and organic dyes due to its beneficial optical properties [21–24]. Ti-TPA-MOF is inactive under visible light due to its 3.65 eV energy gap. Additionally, the rapid recombination of (e−) and (h+) restricts its widespread use [25,26]. Several methods have been proposed to overcome the constraints of Ti-TPA-MOF, such as incorporating metallic nanoparticles, semiconductors, and organic group functionalization. Nevertheless, these remedies are complex, expensive, and potentially risky. Creating composites with core-shell structures can be accomplished by utilizing nanoparticles like TiO2, carbon quantum dots, and ternary nanoparticles, which are both environmentally friendly and widely available, making this method highly effective [27–29].

Quantum dots-MOF composites have recently garnered significant interest and emerged as a hot spot area of research [30,31]. The combination of quantum dots and MOF has excellent potential for technology and scientific exploration. This method successfully addresses the problem of quantum dot aggregation caused by their small size and high surface energy. It also opens new opportunities for innovative applications [29,30,32]. Nanomaterials known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have dimensions ranging from 2 to 10 nm [33]. CQDs are gaining recognition in photo-catalysis due to their eco-friendly properties, non-toxic nature, and ability to modify luminescence wavelength. They also have remarkable optical characteristics, high electron conductivity, and are easily functionalized. Creating a visible-light band gap further enhances their potential in catalytic processes [34,35]. The presence of quantum dots as electron donors or acceptors in CQDs-Ti-TPA-MOF structures can improve their ability to absorb visible light and enhance their photocatalytic activity. Also, CQDs lead to efficient electron-hole separation and transport [36–39].

Yu et al. demonstrated improved photocatalytic activity by creating a CQDs/UiO-66 MOG structure, resulting in a 4.4-fold enhancement in toluene degradation compared to pure UiO-66 MOG [40]. Wei et al. synthesized a composite of CQDs and ZIF-8, which showed the highest removal performance of NO at approximately 43 % [41]. Li et al. fabricated CQDs@MIL-125(Ti), resulting in a remarkable 90 % degradation efficiency of TC within 4 h [18]. Yu-Xuan Li and colleagues developed TiO2/Ti-MIL-125 composites with a core-shell structure, exhibiting greater photocatalytic Cr (VI) reduction performance than virgin TiO2 and pure Ti-MIL-125 [42].

In the current study, Titanium has been used in a distinctive way that sets this study apart from similar endeavors. Titanium is used in both the shell and the core. In the synthesized photocatalytic composite, the shell is composed of Ti-TPA_MOF, which is created from the covalent bond of titanium as a metal and TPA as a ligand. Furthermore, Ti has been employed as the core in the TiO2 nanoparticles. To enhance the absorption of visible light, improve electron conductivity, minimize recombination, and decrease the band gap, a combination of three nanoparticles, namely Cu0.5Zn0.5S, CQDs, and TiO2, was utilized. Triple heterojunction composites synthesized through solvothermal to investigate photocatalytic capability were used for the photo-degradation of gentamicin as a photo-reactor model. In the triple heterojunction composite, the CQDs function as an electron collector, which is crucial role in separating carrier charges. Furthermore, it is expected that TiO2 and Cu0.5Zn0.5S can enhance photocatalytic performance by inhibiting the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs and reducing the energy gap.

Descriptions of the substances utilized to realize the desired idea are presented in the following passage: Dimethyl formamide (Merck, anhydrous, 99.8 %), Citric acid monohydride ((C6H8O7.H2O), Merck, purity ≥99.9 %), Methanol (Merck, anhydrous, 99.9 %), terephthalic acid (TPA, Tondgooyan, purity ≥99 %), Ethylene diamine (Merck, purity ≥99.0 %), Titanium-(IV) isopropoxide (TIP, DAE JUNG, purity ≥98 %), Titanium dioxide(TiO2, Merck), gentamicin (Iran Daru Pharmaceutical Co. in Tehran), zinc nitrate (Zn (NO3)2.6H2O, Merck, purity ≥99.20 %), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, Mw =55,000), copper nitrate (Cu (NO3)2·3H2O, Merck, purity ≥99.0 %), Potassium iodide (KI, Merck, purity ≥99.0 %), p-benzoquinone (BNQ, Merck, purity ≥99.0 %) and isopropanol (IPA, Merck, purity ≥99.0 %).

For examination of crystal structure, X-ray diffraction (XRD), utilizing an X-ray diffractometer (PHILIPS, PW1730, Nederland) within 5–800(2θ), was employed. The UV–visible near infrared absorption spectrometer (SCINCO S_4100, South Korean) was employed to estimate the diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS), which ranges from 200to 900 nm. At ambient temperature, employing an Agilent Cary Eclipes fluorescence spectrophotometer (G9800A, America), photoluminescence (PL) spectra were investigated. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (UHV-XPS, SPECS Phobias 150, Germany) with Al Kα target ray (1486.6 eV) was utilized to examine the surface chemical components of the catalysts. At 25 °C, a Bettersize Nanoptic (plus 90, France) was used to conduct a dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis. To observe the MOFs samples morphology, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Ziess EM900, Germany) employed with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV at 20 °C. An electron paramagnetic resonance ESR spectrometer (Bruker E300, Germany) was utilized to collect the free radicals created during the reaction with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide DMPO.

The core-shell composites were fabricated by the following synthetic route. Specifically, 20 mg of dried TiO2 was dispersed in 2.0 mL of dry methanol and mixed for 15 min. Then, the solutions were added to a dry DMF solution (10.0 mL) containing TPA (0.56 g) and ultrasonically processed for 15 min. After that, the solutions were vigorously stirred for 20 min while slowly and drop by drop injected with 0.5 mL of titanium isopropoxide. The solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined steel autoclave and heated to a temperature of 150 °C for a duration of 20 h. All the procedures were completed in a glove box with nitrogen present. The solution was cooled to room temperature and then washed multiple times with DMF and methanol. Afterward, it was dried at 80 °C overnight.

To produce TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S heterojunction composite, TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF powder (0.25 g) was dispersed in methanol using ultrasonication. The TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF solution was mixed with a prepared solution containing Cu (NO3)2·3H2O and Zn (NO3)2.6H2O in a 1:1 M ratio. The anion solution, consisting of S2− ions, was created by dissolving CH4N2S in double distilled water. The two solutions were combined, and 25 % ammonium hydroxide was gradually added to maintain a pH of 10. The mixture was processed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 150 °C for 180 min. The resulting precipitate was collected and rinsed with methanol. In the second step, the TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S heterojunction composite was dispersed in distilled water with citric acid and dissolved PVP. After the gradual addition of ethylene diamine, the mixture was stirred for 30 min until a yellow color appeared. PVP was introduced to regulate particle size, protect against photo-corrosion, and prevent aggregation of quantum dots. The solution was heated in a Teflon-lined steel autoclave at 180 °C for 90 min. After cooling, the resulting powder was rinsed with methanol and dried overnight at 45 °C. Fig. 1 displays all the synthesis steps.

This study investigated the use of composites as photocatalysts for the degradation of gentamicin in water under visible light. Different amounts of Ti-TPA-MOF, TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF, and CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S were added to a 1000 mL solution. Under ambient conditions, the reaction proceeded in a photoreactor at room temperature, with a pH of 6.5 and a pollutant concentration of 25 ppm. The average intensity of irradiation was measured as 7.82 mW/cm2 (HPCS-6500 spectroradiometer), and the irradiation area was 18.37 cm2. To maximize efficiency, a 60 W jacketed lamp as the visible light source (λ > 400 nm, 5.7 A, 13.5 V) is placed near the photocatalyst in the reaction system. The solution was stirred in the dark for 60 min before irradiation. After centrifugation, 5 mL of the suspension was analyzed using UV–vis spectroscopy at 400 nm at different time intervals. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S was also studied when 25 mg/L gentamicin interfered with pollutants (1 mg/L phenol, acetaminophen, and azithromycin). The stability and recyclability of the CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S composites were also evaluated under visible light.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, as depicted in Fig. 2 , was utilized to ascertain the amorphous or crystalline nature of the nanoparticles, which include pure Ti-TPA-MOF, TiO2@Ti_TPA-MOF, Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods and CQDs/TiO2@Ti_TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S. Comparing the XRD peaks of Ti-TPA-MOF with the core-shell structure of TiO2@Ti-TAP-MOF provides a clear demonstration of the successful synthesis. Upon examining the details presented in Fig. 2, it is apparent that the diffraction peak can be found within the 2θ range of 5–80°. The specific peak positions for Ti-TPA-MOF are identified at 6.5°, 9.5°, 11.54°, 16.46°, 17.88°, and 19.5°. The presence of additional peaks in the XRD pattern of TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF can be attributed to the TiO2 particles located at different angles, namely 25.8°, 38.8°, 49.09°, 55.99°, 63.79°, and 76.29°. The observed peaks suggest the presence of the anatase phase associated with TiO2, while no peaks related to rutile and brookite were detected. Based on the graph, it can be observed that the main peaks of Ti-TPA-MOF have remained consistent as a shell. The core-shell material exhibits a crystal-like structure, evident from the strong peaks observed in its diagram. Furthermore, the presence of distinct peaks in the XRD pattern of Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods suggests the crystalline nature of the structures that are located at 29.04°, 30.44°, 49.24°, 52.04°, and 60.4°. XRD investigation of the triple heterojunction composite exhibited additional peaks due to Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods and CQDs coated on TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF core-shell structure. Even though the composite contains CQDs, there is no discernible peak because the main peak of the CQDs overlaps with the TiO2 main peak at an angle of 25°. The successful combination has been convincingly demonstrated by conforming the XRD diagram peaks of each material individually and in the core-shell and heterojunction structure with Ti-TPA-MOF. The uniform distribution of nanoparticles in Ti-TPA-MOF and the absence of additional peaks indicate the purity of nanoparticles, QDs, and the synthesized core-shell structure. The FWHM values obtained from XRD analysis for the triple composites, Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods, and TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF core-shell structure is 0.1252, 0.4428, and 0.1562 (2Ө), respectively. By applying Scherer's relationship, the crystal sizes of these materials were determined as 66.38, 19.42, and 53.23 nm, respectively. It is worth noting that previous studies have shown that larger crystal sizes have a more positive impact on photocatalytic activity [43]. The determination of the crystallinity percentage was carried out using OriginPro software, with the application of the following expression [44].

The crystallinity percentages of the triple heterojunction composites, core-shell structure, and Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods were determined to be 83 %, 58 %, and 31 %, respectively, through calculations.

The images in Fig. 3 reveal the morphology of pure Ti-TPA-MOF and CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S triple heterojunction composites through TEM analysis. The composition of Ti-TPA-MOF is highlighted in part (a), where the presence of pristine particles is revealed. These particles possess a surface with smoothness and a partially columnar structure. As per part (b), the triple heterojunction composites comprise a core-shell structure consisting of TiO2 as the core and Ti-TPA-MOF as the shell, which has been successfully encapsulated. By coating nanorods of Cu0.5Zn0.5S and CQDs onto TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF, a triple heterojunction composite was successfully fabricated. The lighter background represents the pure Ti-TPA-MOF, whereas the dark coarse dots depict TiO2 and the fine dots represent carbon quantum dots. Additionally, nanorods are representative of Cu0.5Zn0.5S. The images indicate that the shell of pure Ti-TPA-MOF consists of TiO2 as the core. By examining the TEM images, it is evident that the core-shell structure has been successfully formed, and the morphology remains unchanged even after the particles are encapsulated within Ti-TPA-MOF.

By employing XPS, a thorough investigation into the composition of surface elements and their chemical valence state in materials was conducted. The composite of CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S triple heterojunction displays a spectrum of S, Cu, Zn, Ti, and C elements as indicated in Fig. 4. Furthermore, Ti-TPA-MOF solely exhibits the occurrence of elements including Ti, C, and O. The data presented in Fig. 4 (b) reveal that for Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, the binding energy peaks, are 457.95 and 464.45 eV, respectively, as per the provided information. These values propound the attendance of Ti4+ in the Ti-OXO cluster [45,46]. The binding energy peaks, found within the 540.1 eV range, could potentially indicate the presence of various oxygen species such as O 1S, Ti-OXO cluster, CO, and hydroxyl groups in both pure and triple heterojunction composite [46,47]. The triple heterojunction composite exhibits a disparity of around 0.9 and 0.5 eV in the Ti 2p data compared to pure Ti-TPA-MOF. This discrepancy in binding energy could be attributed to the tremendous ionization energy of sulfur in contrast to titanium, leading to a decrease in the binding energy values [48]. The Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 binding energy values, found at 952.1 and 932.1 eV, respectively (Fig. 4 (d)), were associated with the presence of copper. Similarly, the Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 double peaks, observed at 1036.9 and 1021.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 4(e)), were attributed to the presence of zinc. Additionally, the binding energy values at 161.1 and 162.4 eV could be attributed to sulfur, specifically the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 states [49,50]. The number of active species in a material directly affects its photocatalytic activity. The ICP-OES technique was utilized to determine the concentrations of Ti, Cu, and Zn elements in the triple heterojunction composites of CQDS/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S. Additionally, the confirmation of carbon quantum dots in the triple heterojunction composites was achieved by conducting CHN analysis. Table 1 exhibits that the triple heterojunction composite has a higher carbon content than Ti-TPA-MOF and contains around 50 ppm Cu and 48 ppm Zn.

As shown in Fig. 4(f), FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to detect chemical compositions and sample bonding. The peaks between 400 and 800 cm−1 for Ti-TPA-MOF can be contributed to the vibrations of O–Ti–O and the peaks in the range of 1000–1300 cm−1 and 1500–1700 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of CO and asymmetric stretching vibration of–COOH, respectively. All the spectra contain peak located at 3426 cm-1, which is the OH stretching of H2O [51]. In the FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs, it is found that the strong absorption in the range of 3200–3400 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of–OH, which proves that the surface of the CQDs contains –OH [18]. Characteristic peaks for CO, –COOH and–OH are observed at the same wavelength in CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S, and the FT-IR spectroscopic results prove that the CQDs have been successfully anchored to Ti-TPA-MOF. The existence of frequency peaks range at 1000–900 cm−1 is attributed to Cu–O–Ti and C–O–Ti bending vibrations. Ti–O–Ti and Cu–O–Ti bonding was presented at vibrational frequencies of 500–1200 cm−1. The signals at 519 cm−1 are attributed to C–Cu–Ti stretching vibration which indicates the formation of TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S composite [52,53]. The peak of 680 cm−1 is attributed to Zn-O-Ti bonds [54]. Based on the above analysis it can be said that the synthesis of CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S has been successful.

The optical absorption properties of the samples prepared were showcased through the acquisition of DRS spectra. Fig. 5 (a, b) illustrates the optical absorption characteristics of the prepared samples. Ti-TPA-MOF exhibits its peak absorption at approximately 330 nm, lacking any significant light absorption in the visible light range. Contrastingly, triple heterojunction composites demonstrate a vast absorption range from 550 to 610 nm, enabling efficient visible light harvesting. This noteworthy disparity highlights the superior visible light-harvesting capabilities of triple heterojunction composites compared to Ti-TPA-MOF. By incorporating CQDs and Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods into the TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF core-shell structure, the absorption edge of the maximum triple heterojunction composites undergoes a significant alteration. This change signifies a successful interaction between the three components. The alteration significantly boosts the material's capacity to harness visible light and facilitate photocatalytic reactions within the visible light spectrum. Indeed, a distinctive shift in DRS signifies a heightened aptitude for light absorption. Tauc's relation was utilized to compute the optical band gap energy of Ti-TPA-MOF, TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF, Cu0.5Zn0.5S, and CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S, based on the absorption spectra data.

. The band gap values for different materials were determined as follows: Ti-TPA-MOF had a band gap of 3.63 eV, TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF had a band gap of 3.24 eV, Cu0.5Zn0.5S had a band gap of 1.89 eV, and CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S had a band gap of 2.28 eV. The outcomes indicate that the triple heterojunction composite of CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S exhibits a broader absorption edge and a stronger photo-response than Ti-TPA-MOF. This demonstrates that the CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S composite is a highly efficient photocatalyst in UV–visible light. It was proved that adding CQDs and Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods increases the light harvesting capacity of the composites and reduces their band gap.

The effectiveness of a photocatalyst is often compromised when it experiences rapid recombination between the photoexcited electron and hole, resulting in reduced capabilities. This is commonly observed in photocatalysts that are not highly efficient. The recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs results in energy production through PL emission. A more substantial emission peak or increased PL intensity signifies a faster recombination rate, resulting in a reduced duration of electron-hole segregation. Conversely, a diminished PL intensity represents a slower recombination rate, allowing for an extended period of electron-hole segregation [45,59,60]. Photoluminescence measurements were conducted on pure Ti-TPA-MOF and CQDs@TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S to investigate the characteristics of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S triple heterojunction is significantly reduced when compared to the pure Ti-TPA-MOF, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The intense emission peak observed in Ti-TPA-MOF is believed to be a result of the recombining that occurs between electron and hole pairs directly photoexcited from LUMO to HOMO. The suppression of photoluminescence intensity suggests that the recombination of photogenerated carriers has been inhibited and that there is efficient charge transfer between TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF, CQDs, and Cu0.5Zn0.5S. Incorporating TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF with CQDs and Cu0.5Zn0.5S effectively inhibits the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. This is done by offering additional pathways for electron relocation and simplifying the charge immigration process, as evidenced by the findings of this study.

Photocarrier transport plays a vital role in photocatalytic reactions, as previously stated. Photocurrent measurement was employed to examine the migration of photoexcited carriers in Ti-TPA-MOF, TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF, and CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S. The fabricated samples' photocurrent density-time curves are depicted in Fig. 6 (b), accompanied by their swift responses to light on/off. It is evident that when exposed to lamp radiation, the photocurrent density for TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF and CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S exhibits a higher intensity and a faster rate of increase compared to that of Ti-TPA-MOF. Triple heterojunction nanocomposites exhibit a heightened responsiveness to photocurrent, underscoring their superior performance in this area. The ameliorated photocatalytic efficiency can be ascribed to incorporating CQDs and Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods, which facilitate effective electron immigration and consequently inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs. This introduction of CQDs and Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods leads to improved photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the photocurrent's intensity remains consistent even after numerous occurrences of switching the lamp on and off. The study's results indicate that CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S demonstrate remarkable stability in photoelectric chemical reactions. Additionally, it surpasses Ti-TPA-MOF in generating a higher quantity of photogenerated electrons when subjected to identical irradiation conditions. Furthermore, it demonstrates a significantly higher efficacy in electron separation and transfer than Ti-TPA-MOF.

Semiconductor materials in the form of tiny particles or nanocrystals, known as quantum dots (QDs), have diameters that usually range from 1 to 10 nm [61]. The successful fabrication of CQDs, with an average size of 6.05 nm, is depicted in Fig. 7 , as confirmed through DLS analysis. Table 2 further reveals that 90 % of these CQDs are smaller than 7.52 nm, in compliance with the established standards. However, the DLS analysis and nanoparticle size confirm that carbon quantum dots can be synthesized within the desired range by employing a temperature of 180 °C and a time of 90 min.

A more profound comprehension of the separation process between photoexcited electrons and photogenerated holes can be achieved by analyzing the chemical potential values of various components, such as the conduction band (CB), LUMO, valence band (VB), and HOMO. Empirical equations can be utilized to calculate the values of chemical potential.

Moreover, it should be noted that the standard redox potentials for (O2/•O2) and (•OH/OH) are −0.046 ev and + 2.38 ev/NHE, respectively [65–68]. The encountering of light with CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S leads to the harvesting of photons by the photocatalyst, leading to the excitation of electrons and the generation of holes. Based on the data presented in Fig. 8 , it can be observed that CQDs, TiO2, and Cu0.5Zn0.5S exhibit higher conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) positions when compared to Ti-TPA-MOF. Due to its more positive position, the CB of TiO2 and Ti-TPA-MOF can act as a destination for photogenerated electrons in the CB of CQDs and Cu0.5Zn0.5S, facilitating their movement. The creation of holes within the valence band of Ti-TPA-MOF results in the transfer of these holes to the CQDs, TiO2, and Cu0.5Zn0.5S, as their valence band exhibits a more negative charge and is positioned at a higher level. By introducing a new pathway, the photocatalytic performance is greatly improved. This pathway not only extends the lifespan of charges that have migrated, but also effectively prevents the recombination of electron-hole pairs, leading to a significant enhancement in overall performance. This notable advancement brings about a remarkable boost in the efficiency of photocatalysis.

The BET surface of the triple composites decreased compared to pure Ti-TPA-MOF due to the incorporation of nanoparticles, quantum dots, and Cu0.5Zn0.5S nanorods. The outcomes can be observed in Table 3 . Consequently, the absorption of the composites is slightly lower than that of pure MOF. It is essential to acknowledge that the loaded nanoparticles still exhibit some absorption. However, the photocatalytic activity is greatly enhanced due to the increased number of active metal sites, the presence of electron-conducting quantum dots that efficiently capture light, and the nanorods that minimize the energy gap. According to Fig. 9 , once the pressure comes near to atmospheric pressure, the N2 adsorptions of Ti-TPA-MOF and CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S triple heterojunction composite samples at 77 K indicate type 1 behavior. Thus, synthesized photocatalysts are microporous materials [18,69].

Unlike Ti-TPA-MOF and TiO2, the photocatalytic triple heterojunction composites exhibit a unique behavior when exposed to visible light. CQDs and Cu0.5Zn0.5S readily get excited by visible light, leading to the creation of electrons and holes due to their unique energy gap in the visible-light range. In the triple heterojunction composites, the migration of photogenerated electrons in nanoparticles to Ti-OXO clusters containing Ti4+ in the Ti-TPA-MOF results in the transformation of Ti4+ to Ti3+. Superoxide radicals (•O2 −) are generated by exchanging electrons between O2 and Ti3+ ions. This occurs because of oxygen molecules sticking to the porous Ti-TPA-MOF and Ti3+’s ability to undergo reduction. After being oxidized, the Ti3+ ions are transformed into Ti4+ ions. CQDs play a crucial role in enabling the efficient collection and transfer of electrons, while TiO2 acts as an obstacle to recombination, and Cu0.5Zn0.5S is employed to decrease the band gap. Consequently, the surface electron-holes present in the photocatalyst get separated efficiently, acting as photoreactive sites that produce remarkably reactive species capable of breaking down pollutants through photodegradation. When photoexcited electrons from the conduction band (CB) of CQDs, TiO2, and Cu0.5Zn0.5S are transferred to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of Ti-TPA-MOF, charge separation occurs. The existence of holes in the valence band of the nanoparticle permits the oxidation of water, ultimately yielding hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Subsequently, photoexcited electrons are absorbed by O2, which is soluble in the reaction medium, resulting in the formation of •O2 − and •OH. The degrading free radicals, •O2 − and •OH, effectively convert contaminants into other products. It is essential to mention that the chemical potential of CB nanoparticles used in the composites is comparatively more negative than that of O2/•O2 − (which is equivalent to −0.046 ev). This difference in potential facilitates the reduction of oxygen molecules and the production of •O2 − radicals. Moreover, the chemical potential of VB needs to be more positive to oxidize H2O to •OH at a potential of +2.38 eV. The process of photodegradation can be described in the following manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessing gentamicin photodegradation under varying conditions

ce:section.ce:para: During the 80-min irradiation period, gentamicin degradation remained consistent without photocatalyst. Photodegradation experiments were conducted at various doses to compare the photocatalytic performance of Ti-TPA-MOF, TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF, and CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S in the degradation of gentamicin. Results showed CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S composites had higher photodegradation ability than pure Ti-TPA-MOF and TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF composites. This is due to the appropriate energy gap, allowing excitation by visible light. Among composites, CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S with 150 mg demonstrated the highest photodegradation efficiency of approximately 95 % after 80 min of visible light exposure. This superior performance is attributed to a narrow band gap, reduced electron-hole recombination, increased active sites, and higher current density.

The role of pH on the photodegradation efficiency of gentamicin was evaluated in the pH range 3–9, at constant nanocomposite dosage (150 mg/L) and constant gentamicin concentration (25 mg/L). For triple heterojunction composites, different pH has various physiochemical properties like surface charge, thus influencing the adsorptive interactions between the gentamicin and the surface of triple heterojunction. Fig. 10 (b) shows the effects of pH on the photodegradation of gentamicin by triple heterojunction. Meanwhile, gentamicin adsorption on the triple heterojunction strongly depends on the solution pH values. The optimal pH for achieving maximum photodegradation efficiency was found to be 6.5. The degradation of gentamicin is considerably more pronounced at a pH of 9, but it shows a significant decrease as the pH value decreases from 6.5 to 3. This suggests that the surface affinity of gentamicin for the triple heterojunction is reduced at lower pH levels. As shown in Fig. 10 (c)), triple heterojunction has an overall negative surface charge at pH 5.6–10.0. As the pH value increases, the zeta potentials are more negative. With the increase of pH values in solution, an enhanced adsorption capacity can be explained by the predominant electrostatic attraction between opposite positively charged gentamicin molecules and negatively charged triple heterojunction. The electrostatic interaction between the surface of triple heterojunction and gentamicin can account for the difference in photodegradation efficiency in different pH solutions. Since the pKa value of gentamicin is 9, gentamicin was positively charged below this value [74,75], whereas the photocatalyst was negatively charged above the pH point zero charge (pHPZC ∼ 5.6). Thus, the optimum condition could be achieved in the range pHPZC < pH < pKa [76,77], where the positively charged gentamicin and negatively charged photocatalyst should easily attract each other, which correlates to enhanced adsorption of the gentamicin onto triple heterojunction and thus increased degradation efficiency. It is important to highlight that obtaining an alkaline pH in a water-based solution necessitates a substantial quantity of alkalizing agent. Due to the large volume of wastewater containing pollutants, adding substantial amounts of alkalizing substances can lead to pollution. Hence, a pH of 6.5 was determined as the optimal condition to balance efficiency and environmental concerns.

The enhancement in photodegradation performance is evident as the dosage of photocatalysts increases, owing to the rise in active metal sites. However, it is crucial to note that the photocatalyst dosage should be increased only up to an optimal value. The graph clearly illustrates that as the photocatalyst dosage is raised from 50 to 150 mg, the degradation of gentamicin intensifies at a concentration of 25 ppm and pH level of 6.5. This can be attributed to the heightened number of active sites resulting from the increased photocatalyst dose. The degradation efficiency diminishes as the catalyst dose is increased from 150 to 200 mg. The decline in degradation efficiency observed can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, as the concentration of the photocatalyst rises, the suspension becomes more turbid, causing a decline in the penetration of visible light. This, in turn, lowers the efficiency of photoactivation. Secondly, the excess CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S in the reaction solution can result in the inactivation of photogenerated free radicals through collision. Therefore, when the quantity of photocatalyst is increased beyond the optimum value, it hampers the effectiveness of the degradation process [78]. The degradation efficiency of gentamicin by the current composite was compared with the other published works as indicated in Table 5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation kinetic and photo-mineralization performance assessment
ce:section.ce:para:
The efficacy of the photodegradation process was assessed by investigating the kinetics of gentamicin degradation when exposed to visible light with the aid of the newly created photocatalysts. The pseudo-first-order kinetics was considered for this purpose [23].

indicates gentamicin concentrations at a specific time. The relative photodegradation rate constant defined as k and time represented as t [82,83]. Plotting − ln

as a function of time using Eq. (13) yields the results shown in Fig. 11 (a). By observing the linear relationship between the slope of k and − ln
C f

, it can be concluded that the photocatalytic reaction follows a pseudo-first-order kinetic model. Obtained k encountering to visible light irradiation for gentamicin photodegradation using CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S triple heterojunctions composite, TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF and Ti-TPA-MOF with the amount of 150 mg was equal to 0.032, 0.0044 and 0.0021 min−1. According to the obtained results, the efficiency of photodegradation by CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF /Cu0.5Zn0.5S is more than other as-synthesized samples due to having a higher k value. The half-life time, which was calculated as 22 min, is obtained according to the following equation:

The mineralization of the decomposed products stands out as a notable phenomenon photocatalysis. The measurement of total organic carbon (TOC) was employed to validate the effectiveness of CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S triple heterojunction composites in the mineralization process of gentamicin. According to Fig. 11 (b), mineralization efficiency increased as the photoreaction time increased. The mineralization efficiency was 80.1, 16.6 and 6.94 % for gentamicin in the presence of CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S, TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF, and Ti-TPA-MOF, while the photodegradation efficiency was significantly higher for this pollutant. The existence of intermediates (side products) formed within the photocatalytic operation is accountable for the observed variation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radicals trapping test and interference effect
ce:section.ce:para: Superoxide (•O2 −), holes (h+), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are well-known to be the highly reactive species involved in the photodegradation reaction process [84]. Trapping tests were utilized to investigate the efficacy and contribution of various active species in the photodegradation process using CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S photocatalytic composites. To capture photo-created h+, •O2 −, and •OH species, respectively, potassium iodide (KI), p-benzoquinone (BNQ), and isopropanol (IPA) were chosen as radical scavengers in this study [57]. The impact of different scavengers on the photoreaction process is depicted in Fig. 12 (c). Gentamicin photodegradation declined significantly after the addition of BNQ and IPA. The addition of KI did not result in a significant change, indicating that hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide (•O2 −) were the predominant reactive free radicals in the photocatalytic process. To identify the free radicals generated during the photodegradation process, the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique was employed, with DMPO serving as the spin-trapping compound. Fig. 12 (a, b) confirms the significant contribution of •O2 − and •OH in the photo-degradation process when CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S is present. Unlike pure Ti-TPA-MOF, which exhibits a weak signal upon exposure to light irradiation, observing distinct firm peaks underscores the role played by •O2 − and •OH in the degradation process. The outcomes were strikingly consistent with those obtained from the trapping experiments.

To examine the interference effect, the study focused on phenolic and pharmaceutical pollutants that had a pKa value higher than the point of zero charge (pzc) of the photocatalyst. These pollutants were chosen to compete with gentamicin, which has a pKa value of 9. Specifically, azithromycin with a pKa of ∼8.5 [85], phenol with a pKa of 9.5 [76], and acetaminophen with a pKa of 9.38 [76] were included in the investigation. The photocatalyst exhibits a negative surface charge when the pH value is in the range where pzc < pH < pKa, while the pollutant has a positive charge. As a result, an attractive force is established between them, leading to their easy attraction [76]. The photocatalytic composite decreased degradation efficiency to 66, 60, and 81 %, after 80 min of interaction when phenol, acetaminophen, and azithromycin were present. The performance of photodegradation in the presence of pollutants can be ascribed to various factors, including the size of the pollutant molecules, the magnitude of the surface charge, and the reactivity with free radicals [78]. Acetaminophen molecules are smaller than gentamicin molecules, and on the other hand, phenols react easily and very quickly with hydroxyl radicals. For this reason, the presence of phenol and acetaminophen leads to a further decrease in the degradation efficiency of gentamicin.

The practical process requires photocatalytic stability and recyclability, which were investigated in this study. The durability experiments involved reusing the CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S photocatalytic composites for gentamicin degradation under visible light. The results showed that the photocatalyst remained consistent throughout five cycles, with only a 7 % decrease in photocatalytic activity. This indicates that the photocatalysts maintain stability and efficiency in aqueous reaction environments, thanks to the preservation of their optical properties and strength provided by the introduced nanoparticles in the core-shell structures and triple heterojunction composites. These composites have a high efficiency of 95 % in breaking down antibiotics under low intensity (60 W) and in a shorter time (80 min) compared to other photocatalysts mentioned (Table 6 ). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to determine the crystalline phase of CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S triple heterojunction composites, and the results were compared with the original photocatalysts. The obtained figure reveals that specific peaks, initially present, have vanished or weakened, while the prominent peaks remain robust (Fig. 13 (b)). Upon closer examination of the diminished peaks, it was observed that they are associated with Cu0.5Zn0.5S and Ti-TPA-MOF. This occurrence may adversely impact the number of active sites and consequently lead to a partial reduction in photocatalytic activity.

Important factors are involved in choosing a suitable technology for wastewater treatment, such as economy, scale, operation (including maintenance, control, and safety), and robustness. Among the mentioned factors, costs are much more critical. Electrical energy constitutes a substantial portion of the operational expenses associated with photodegradation, breaking down organic pollutants through light exposure requires a significant amount of energy. Accordingly, providing values based on the amount of electrical energy consumption can be overly helpful. This energy is quantified as the number of kilowatt-hours (kWh) needed to achieve this reduction in a volume of 1 m3 at low pollutant concentration. The following relation is used in this work to calculate how much electrical energy is used to photodegrade gentamicin by a photocatalyst that follows first-order reaction kinetics in a batch photoreactor.
(15)
E EO

P ∗ t ∗ 1000
V ∗ 60 ∗ log

where
E EO
is electrical energy per order, P is the inlet power (kW) to the advanced oxidation process system, t is the reaction time (min), and V is the volume of deionized water (L) in the batch photoreactor,
C i
and
C f
initial and final concentration of gentamicin, respectively [91,92]. The electrical energy calculated for the photocatalysts utilized in the photodegradation of gentamicin is presented in Table 7 , employing the specified equation. The estimated values show that the CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S photocatalyst consumes the minor electrical energy, which may be attributed to its superior photocatalytic activity, lower energy gap, and lesser photoexcited electron-hole recombination. Thus, low electrical energy consumption lowers the expense of photocatalytic operations. It is worth noting that visible light serves as a simulation of sunlight. Consequently, when there is a lack of sunlight irradiation, it becomes necessary to employ lamps emitting visible spectrum to activate the electrons in triple heterojunction photocatalytic composites.

Briefly, CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S triple heterojunction composites based on core-shell structure were synthesized by loading Ti-TPA-MOF on a TiO2 surface through a solvothermal method. Compared with pure Ti-TPA-MOF and TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF as a photocatalyst, CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S triple heterojunction composites showed more abundant active sites, more impressible separation of electron-hole pairs, quenching of recombination and narrower band gap. CQDs/TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF/Cu0.5Zn0.5S photocatalysts exhibited superior photocatalytic efficiency in the degradation of gentamicin compared to pure Ti-TPA-MOF and TiO2@Ti-TPA-MOF core-shell composites. The utilization of Ti-TPA-MOF, combined with TiO2, CQDs, and Cu0.5Zn0.5S, demonstrates a modified approach that offers numerous benefits for wastewater treatment. By leveraging the pollutant absorption capabilities of Ti-TPA-MOF, the recombination inhibition properties of TiO2, and the light harvesting and energy gap narrowing attributes of CQDs and Cu0.5Zn0.5S, this method shows great potential in enhancing optical stability and effectively treating wastewater.
Khalil Abbassian: Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Mohammad Rahmani: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, supervision.
